title: First Lava Tube Found on Venus—Bigger Than Any on Earth
summary:
Venus, often called Earth's "sister planet," has finally given up one of its secrets: a massive underground lava tube. Scientists discovered it by taking a fresh look at radar images captured by NASA's Magellan probe in the early 1990s. The data revealed a collapsed "skylight"—a hole where the roof of the lava tube had caved in—near a broad volcano called Nyx Mons. Further analysis showed the skylight is about 150 meters deep and opens into an empty lava tube at least 375 meters deep. The researchers estimate the tube could be up to one kilometer wide, making it larger than any lava tube found on Earth or Mars.

"It is remarkable that we are still extracting new insights from Magellan data," said Anna Gülcher, a planetary scientist at the University of Freiburg who was not involved in the study. The research, published in Nature Communications on February 9, 2026, is the first detection of a lava tube on Venus.

The discovery is significant for two reasons. First, it shows that Venus's volcanic landscape is more complex than previously thought. Second, it will help guide two upcoming missions: NASA's VERITAS (scheduled to launch before June 2031) and the European Space Agency's EnVision (expected later that year). Both spacecraft will carry advanced radar instruments capable of imaging Venus's surface at much higher resolution than Magellan.

But don't expect astronauts to explore this lava tube anytime soon. Venus's surface pressure is 93 times greater than Earth's, and temperatures are hot enough to melt regular electronics. The tube remains a tantalizing target for robotic exploration, however. Lava tubes on the moon and Mars have been proposed as potential shelters for astronauts, protecting them from radiation and meteorites. On Venus, such a shelter would need to withstand extreme conditions.

The finding also raises a mystery: Why is this lava tube so large? Venus has similar gravity to Earth, yet the tube rivals those found on the moon, where lower gravity makes large tubes easier to form. Scientists are eager to find more lava tubes on Venus to understand this puzzle.
why_it_matters: This discovery reminds us that old data can hold new secrets, and it sets the stage for a new era of Venus exploration, where robotic landers and rovers might one day explore underground shelters, reshaping our understanding of Earth's sister planet.

keywords.explanation: A tunnel formed under the surface by flowing lava, often left behind empty after the eruption ends.
A hole in the roof of a lava tube where the ground has collapsed, exposing the empty space below.
A NASA spacecraft that mapped Venus using radar in the early 1990s.
A planned NASA mission to Venus that will map the surface with advanced radar.
A planned European Space Agency mission to study Venus's surface and atmosphere.
The force that pulls objects toward a planet's center; it affects how large caves or tubes can form.

questions.question: What did scientists discover on Venus?

Near which volcano was the skylight found?

options: Because the tube is too narrow
Because Venus has crushing pressure and extreme heat
Because the tube is on the dark side of Venus
Because the tube is flooded with water
questions.correct_answer: Because Venus has crushing pressure and extreme heat

questions.question: What mystery does this lava tube raise?
options: Why it is so large despite Venus's Earth-like gravity
Why it is smaller than expected
Why it is located at the equator
questions.correct_answer: Why it is so large despite Venus's Earth-like gravity

background_read: Venus is often called Earth's sister planet because of its similar size and mass, but its surface is hellish: temperatures around 460°C and atmospheric pressure 93 times that of Earth.

The Magellan probe (1990–1994) used synthetic aperture radar to see through the thick cloud cover and map 98% of Venus's surface at a resolution of about 100 meters. Its data is still being mined for discoveries.

Lava tubes are known on Earth, the Moon, and Mars. They form when the outer surface of a lava flow cools and hardens while the molten interior continues flowing, eventually leaving a hollow tunnel. Their size depends on gravity and volcanic activity.

Two upcoming missions – NASA's VERITAS (launch before June 2031) and ESA's EnVision (later in 2031) – will carry modern radar that can image Venus's surface at much higher resolution than Magellan, potentially revealing more lava tubes.
Article_Structure: LEAD: First detection of a lava tube on Venus – larger than any on Earth or Mars.
DISCOVERY METHOD: Reanalysis of radar data from NASA's Magellan probe (1990s).
└─ Feature: Collapsed skylight near Nyx Mons volcano.
MEASUREMENTS: Skylight depth ~150 m; tube depth >375 m; estimated width up to 1 km.
├─ Shows Venus's volcanic landscape is more complex than thought.
└─ Guides upcoming missions VERITAS (NASA) and EnVision (ESA) – both with advanced radar.
CONSTRAINTS: Venus surface pressure and temperature make manned exploration impossible; robotic exploration is a possibility for the future.
MYSTERY: Why is the tube so large given Venus's Earth-like gravity? Typically larger tubes form in lower gravity (Moon). Scientists hope to find more tubes to solve this.

perspectives.description: Planetary scientists gain a new target for future missions and a deeper understanding of Venus's volcanic processes. The discovery also validates the continued value of archival data from past missions.
The extreme environment of Venus means direct human or even robotic exploration inside the tube is decades away. The tube's existence may raise unrealistic expectations for near-term exploration.
The size anomaly challenges assumptions about lava tube formation under similar gravity conditions. This could lead to revised models of volcanic processes both on Venus and on other rocky bodies.

As VERITAS and EnVision map Venus at higher resolution, more lava tubes are likely to be found. Future robotic missions might drop probes into skylights, using the tubes as natural shelters from radiation – a technique that could also be applied on the Moon and Mars.
